figures.gloss: The unknowable source from whom the world emanates and to whom nothing can be depicted or housed — Circassians built no temples and carved no images of him, praying instead under the open sky. His name opens nearly every traditional Adyghe blessing, yet he is petitioned through the world he pours out rather than approached directly.
figures.facet: A high god defined by what may not be done to him — no image, no house, no priest-caste — so that the revival movement can read Habze as an aniconic monotheism without forcing the old material.
figures.provenance: Interiano (1502) on Circassian religion; Sultan Khan-Girey, 'Notes on Circassia' (1836); Amjad Jaimoukha, 'The Circassians: A Handbook' (2001).

figures.gloss: The unwritten code that governs everything from a guest's seat to a war's conduct — etiquette, honor, hospitality, deference to elders — administered not by clergy but by the thamade, the presiding elder, and by custom-law councils. It is simultaneously ethics, law, and cosmology: to live by Khabze is to live in the grain of the world Tha emanated.
figures.facet: A tradition whose sacred center is a code of conduct rather than a creed — which is why post-Soviet and diaspora revival could rebuild the religion around national identity: keeping Khabze IS the practice.
figures.provenance: Khan-Girey (1836); Jaimoukha (2001); post-Soviet Khabzist movement literature (Kabardino-Balkaria and Adygea, 1990s-2010s).

figures.gloss: Worship conducted in hallowed groves and under ancient trees: an elder speaks the blessing bareheaded, an animal is offered, and the round of makhsyma (the fermented millet-and-honey drink) and shared meat binds the community before Tha and the patron powers of the place.
figures.facet: The grove replaces the temple entirely — sanctity is a property of living terrain, not of built enclosure — which let the rite survive Islamization quietly, as picnic-like village feasts under the same trees.
figures.provenance: 19th-c. ethnography of the Adyghe (Khan-Girey, 1836; Shora Nogmov, 1840s); Jaimoukha (2001) on surviving and revived grove feasts.

figures.gloss: The smith-god of the Nart sagas, master of the forge who works iron with his bare hands, tempers the hero Sosruko in his tongs, and gives the Narts their first tools — the sickle among them — so that craft itself descends from his anvil.
figures.facet: The Circassian smith is a full culture-bringer rather than a court artisan: agriculture's basic implement is his invention, putting the forge at the origin of the sown field.
figures.provenance: Circassian Nart corpus (Hadaghatl'a Asker's collected 'Nartxer', 1968-71); John Colarusso, 'Nart Sagas from the Caucasus' (2002).

figures.gloss: The Nart hero born of stone — quickened when a shepherd's desire struck a boulder, cut free and tempered in Tlepsh's forge until his body was steel everywhere the tongs did not grip. Small, dark, and cunning, he wins by wit what the giant Narts win by strength, and steals fire back from the ogre for the freezing war-band; his untempered knees are his death, sheared by the hurled wheel Zhan-Sharkh.
figures.facet: Fire-thief, stone-born, and vulnerable only at the seam the forge missed — the Circassian telling fuses trickster and tempered-hero into one body whose flaw is a manufacturing defect, not a moral one.
figures.provenance: Circassian Nart corpus (Hadaghatl'a, 1968-71); Colarusso (2002), where the Sosruko cycle anchors the collection.

figures.gloss: The mother of the Narts — ageless, wise, and the actual governing intelligence of the war-band, who raises Sosruko from the stone-birth, brews the first drink, and untangles by counsel what the heroes cannot cut by sword. The sagas make her beauty and her judgment equally proverbial.
figures.facet: A matriarch who runs a heroic society without holding its titles — the corpus's quiet argument that the Nart world is administered from the hearth, not the saddle.

edges.note: The unknowable source and the livable code: Tha does not legislate — the Khabze is how the world he emanated behaves, ethics as cosmology.
Typological seam only: two supreme, imageless, temple-less high powers over a customary-law society — no linguistic or historical descent claimed across the Caucasus/steppe line.
This shard's contribution to the cosmic-order joint: the right-order carried entirely as unwritten etiquette and custom-law, with elders — not priests or texts — as its living medium.
One epos, two peoples: Circassian Sosruko is the same Nart hero as Ossetian Soslan/Sosryko — the shared North Caucasus Nart corpus is documented, corpus-level common inheritance.
Satanaya is Satana of the Ossetian Nart cycle — the same matriarch presiding over both tellings of the shared epic.
Neighboring North Caucasus grove-sanctities — worship under living trees instead of a built temple; areal kinship between adjacent peoples, no derivation shown.
Caucasus kin: two remote high gods over working pantheons among neighboring mountain peoples — areal kinship across unrelated language families (Nakh / Northwest Caucasian), no descent claimed.

dossier.essence: The ethnic religion-and-way of the Circassians (Adyghe) of the northwest Caucasus and, since the expulsion of 1864, of their far larger diaspora in Turkey and the Levant. Its two poles are Tha, the unknowable high god approached without temples, images, or priests, and the Khabze, the unwritten code of etiquette, honor, and hospitality that orders all of life; post-Soviet and diaspora revival movements (Adyghe Khabze) have recodified the pair as the national faith of a scattered people.
dossier.cosmos: The world emanates from Tha, who is never depicted or housed and is prayed to under the open sky, while patron powers — Tlepsh at the forge, Shible in the thunder — administer its working parts. The Nart sagas carry the mythology: the stone-born trickster-hero Sosruko, the matriarch Satanaya, and the war-band whose feats account for fire, tools, and the sown field. Cosmological speculation is otherwise thin by design; the tradition's center of gravity is right conduct within the emanated order, not a map of its far side.
dossier.practice: Worship is communal and lay: an elder prays bareheaded in a sacred grove or under an ancient tree, an animal is offered, and the round of makhsyma (the fermented millet-and-honey drink) and shared meat binds the gathering. Daily religion is the Khabze itself — the sanctity of the guest, deference to elders, the authority of the thamade at every table — administered by custom-law councils rather than clergy. Islamization (17th-19th centuries) overlaid but did not erase the code, and revival movements in Kabardino-Balkaria, Adygea, and the diaspora keep both grove feast and custom-law alive.
dossier.canon: None written: the oral Nart corpus (collected in Hadaghatl'a Asker's multi-volume 'Nartkher', 1968-71, and translated in Colarusso 2002) and the unwritten Khabze itself are the tradition's twin texts.
dossier.reflects: The religious impulse as pure normativity — a tradition whose scripture is a code of conduct, holding that keeping the custom-law of the community IS keeping faith with the source of the world.
sources: John Colarusso, 'Nart Sagas from the Caucasus' (2002)
Sultan Khan-Girey, 'Zapiski o Cherkesii' [Notes on Circassia] (1836; first published 1978)

links.cite: John Colarusso, 'Nart Sagas from the Caucasus' (2002)
